questions.question: When a program calls read(fd, buf, 4096) and the data is already in the page cache, how many copies of the data are created before the program can access buf[0]?
options: Zero — the program accesses the page cache directly
One — the kernel copies from the page cache to the user buffer
Two — once into the page cache, once into the user buffer
Three — disk to kernel, kernel to page cache, page cache to user
questions.explanation: Even with the page cache hit, read() still copies data from the kernel's page cache buffer into the user-space buf. This is the fundamental overhead that mmap and zero-copy techniques aim to eliminate.

questions.question: A process mmap's a 10 GB file with MAP_PRIVATE, then writes to 100 distinct pages (4 KB each). How much additional physical memory is consumed by COW?
options: 10 GB — the entire file is copied
100 × 4 KB = 400 KB — only modified pages are copied
10 GB minus 100 × 4 KB — the original is freed
Zero — MAP_PRIVATE writes modify the file in place
questions.explanation: COW allocates a new physical page only when a page is written. The 100 modified pages each get a private 4 KB copy (400 KB total), while the remaining ~2,547,964 pages continue sharing the original.

questions.question: When is mmap SLOWER than read() for file access?
options: When the file is small and accessed once — mmap setup cost exceeds read's copy
When the file is in the page cache — read is always faster in that case
When using MAP_SHARED — shared mappings are always slower than read
When the file is on an SSD — mmap only helps on HDDs
questions.explanation: mmap has significant setup cost (VMA creation, page table manipulation). For small files accessed once, this overhead exceeds the cost of a single memcpy. mmap also loses for random access on files larger than RAM due to unpredictable page faults.

questions.question: What does sendfile() eliminate that read()+write() does not?
options: Disk I/O — sendfile doesn't read from disk
The copy from kernel page cache to user buffer and back to kernel socket buffer
Network overhead — sendfile uses a different network protocol
File descriptor overhead — sendfile doesn't use file descriptors
questions.explanation: read()+write() requires two copies through user space: disk→page cache→user buf→socket buffer. sendfile() keeps data in kernel space: page cache→socket buffer (or directly to NIC with scatter-gather DMA), eliminating user-space involvement entirely.

questions.question: Why do huge pages (2 MB instead of 4 KB) improve performance for large memory allocations?
options: They reduce the number of page faults to zero
They reduce TLB misses by having fewer page table entries to track
They make memcpy faster by processing 512× more data per instruction
They bypass the page cache and read directly from disk
questions.explanation: A 1 GB allocation needs 262,144 entries with 4 KB pages but only 512 with 2 MB huge pages. Fewer entries means fewer TLB misses (each miss costs 20-40 cycles), which is the dominant cost in page-table-heavy workloads.

questions.question: With MSG_ZEROCOPY on a TCP socket, after calling sendmsg(), when is it safe to modify the send buffer?
options: Immediately — the kernel makes its own copy before sendmsg returns
Only after receiving a completion notification on the socket's error queue
After calling close() on the socket
After waiting at least 1 millisecond for the NIC to DMA the data
questions.explanation: MSG_ZEROCOPY pins user pages for direct DMA by the NIC. The kernel sends a completion notification via recvmsg(MSG_ERRQUEUE) only after the NIC finishes transmitting. Modifying the buffer before this notification corrupts the data being sent.
